turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument next in Case No. 14簫7505, Hurst v. Florida. Mr. Waxman.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Under Florida law, Timothy Hurst will go to his death despite the fact that a judge, not a jury, made the factual finding that rendered 簫簫 rendered him eligible for death. That violates the Sixth Amendment under Ring. In Florida, and Florida alone, what authorizes imposition of the death penalty is a finding of fact by the Court of an aggravating factor, a finding that the trial judge makes independently, and, quote, "notwithstanding the jury's recommendation as to sentence." Now, the State here contends that capital sentencing juries make implicit findings that satisfy the Sixth Amendment under Ring, which the trial judge then simply ratifies. That is wrong. Whatever the jury's recommendation might imply about the specified aggravating factors, the Florida Supreme Court has repeatedly rejected the notion that the jury's verdict is anything other than advisory. Florida law entrusts the factual findings of aggravators to the judge alone, who may do so on the basis of evidence that the jury never heard, and aggravators that the jury was never presented with.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Is there ever a case in which the jury found aggravators and recommended the death sentence, and the judge reversed that finding?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: There may well be. This is principally a case about the finding of death eligibility, not sentence selection. I 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, I mean, either 簫簫 either way, is 簫簫 is there 簫簫 is there 簫簫

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: 簫簫 is there ever a case in which the jury did not find an aggravating circumstance, but the judge did?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, we don't ever know what the jury found about any of the specified aggravating circumstances. The only thing that the jury tells the judge is, we recommend life簫slash簫death by a vote of X versus Y.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Right. But they can't recommend death unless they find the aggravator, right?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, no. No. As a matter of State law, that's not correct. They can't recommend death unless seven of them each believe that some aggravator is satisfied.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: But the Florida Supreme Court 簫簫 and this is a 簫簫 this is a 簫簫 another Ring problem here. The Florida Supreme Court has recognized that where two aggravators are presented, it is impossible to know, even if a simple majority agreed on a single aggravator.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, that's a common feature, though, of jury deliberations. Let's say an aggravator is whether the murder is 簫簫 is particularly heinous. And it can be for 簫簫 for a number of factors: One, the victim is a 簫簫 a 簫簫 a juvenile, so maybe three jurors find that. Or an officer was also killed, or it was in the commission 簫簫 in the course of another felony. In a typical case, a finding that the murder was heinous, you have no idea whether the juror 簫簫 jury as a whole made that determination, or if there were 12 different reasons.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, Florida, and 簫簫 Florida is the only State 簫簫 the only death penalty State and therefore the only State that does not require or permit the jury to be told that it has to agree. And in all other States it's unanimous, but even has to 簫簫 cannot even be told that a majority have to agree as to the existence of one of the specified aggravating factors.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, but isn't that true 簫簫 I mean, taking it even out of the death penalty context, that's true with every jury determination. You could have the jury determining that the 簫簫 the 簫簫 the 簫簫 you know, the 簫簫 the person didn't commit the offense because his alibi was good, or because, you know, somebody else did it, or, you know, any number of 12 different reasons that they think he was not guilty. It doesn't 簫簫

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: It 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: 簫簫 have to be agreement by the jury on a 簫簫 on the particular basis for their verdict.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: We're talking here, Mr. Chief Justice, about elements of the crime. And as this Court explained in Ring, the existence of a statutory aggravating factor is an element of a death簫eligible crime. And can anybody imagine a world, which would be the analogue in Florida, if the jury at the guilt/innocence phase of any trial, a shoplifting trial, were told, now, look. I'm the one who will decide whether the defendant is or isn't guilty as a matter of law, and 簫簫 and eligible for punishment. But I'd like your input on what you 簫簫 whether you think each of the specified elements is or isn't satisfied. I mean, that 簫簫 nobody would stand for an argument like that 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I'm not sure. You 簫簫 you 簫簫

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: 簫簫 or a system like that.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Are you sure that 簫簫 that if you have a crime that can be satisfied by various elements, the jury has to agree upon the specific element that satisfies it?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: The jury 簫簫 if they are distinct elements, and this 簫簫 this implies the Schad point that the State is raising: If the State, consistent with a long historical tradition and a finding of equal culpability, chooses to permit a particular element in Schad, it was premeditation, or the mental state to be satisfied either by premeditation or by felony murder, that's fine. But that is not the Florida system. Florida requires as a matter of law 簫簫 and the Florida Supreme Court has said this over and over again 簫簫 that a defendant is eligible for death only if the trial judge finds as fact, beyond a reasonable doubt, that a particular statutory aggravator exists. And I submit, even if that were not the case, extending Schad, which held that in light of a hundred簫and簫fifty簫year history of States including in the mental element for first degree murder, either felony murder or premeditation, that combining those two elements didn't satisfy the death penalty. None of that is here. This is a question of the Sixth Amendment and the Eighth Amendment. No State ever has said that the jury can just decide some model of aggravation. They just 簫簫 they don't agree on the specific element, and that would violate, I think, the Sixth and Eighth Amendment precedents.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I 簫簫 I 簫簫 I would think that the 簫簫 I would think just the opposite, that 簫簫that the necessity of finding the elements of the crime goes all the way back into the mists of history. And this necessity of finding an aggravator 簫簫 aggravating factor, we made it up, right? I mean, that's just recent Supreme Court law.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Just 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And so if 簫簫 if even one of them should be satisfiable by simply finding the generic conclusion rather than agreeing upon the 簫簫 the particular species at issue, I would think it's 簫簫 it's 簫簫 it's the latter, rather than the former.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Justice Scalia, I'm reminded of your separate opinion 簫簫 I think it was in Walton v. Arizona 簫簫 where you were choosing between two things that you didn't particularly like, and one of them was the fact that the Court had made, recently or not, had made a finding of a 簫簫 beyond a reasonable doubt, a factual finding of a specified aggravating factor an element of the crime. And whether it's recent, whether the court should or shouldn't have done it, it has. And it 簫簫 under Ring, it is just like any other element of the crime. And on the Schad point, I think the other thing I would have said is, the Florida Supreme Court 簫簫 and I'll refer the case 簫簫 the Court to the Bevel case 簫簫 the Florida Supreme Court has said that the 16 aggravating factors that it 簫簫 that makes one eligible for death are vastly incommensurate, in terms of relative levels of 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Mr. Waxman 簫簫

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: 簫簫 moral ability, opposite of the predicate of Schad.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Could I ask you this about Ring? I wasn't on the Court at the time of Ring, so could you tell me if Ring is entitled to greater weight as a precedent than, let's say, Gregg v. Georgia and the other cases upholding the constitutionality of the death penalty?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Oh, I'm not 簫簫 I 簫簫 I wouldn't be prepared to say 簫簫 to assign weight to either of them. I think Ring is certainly predicated on Gregg, to Justice Scalia's point. If Gregg hadn't decided that 簫簫 that there has to be a determinate, specific appellate reviewable narrowing of trial 簫簫 sentencing jury's discretion, Ring wouldn't come up, because an aggravating factor wouldn't be an element.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Do you think this scheme, assuming we agree with Justice Scalia, that you don't really need unanimity, would this still be good law under Apodaca 簫簫 Apodaca 簫簫

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 the case that said that we 簫簫 we needed a unanimous jury, but, you know, nine out of twelve is okay? Do you 簫簫 do you think seven out of five is okay?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: I hope it was clear from our brief that we think 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It's not.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: 簫簫 nine out of five is not okay. It doesn't require this Court to overrule Apodaca, which 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: We're not required to do anything. We could just say it's not the functional equivalent. But is 簫簫

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But is it still good law?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, six 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Shouldn't we overrule it?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: I 簫簫 we think, for the reasons stated in our brief, you should overrule it. And particularly in the Eighth Amendment context where the question is death, the jury should be unanimous. I mean, there is no other State that permits anyone to be sentenced for death other than a unanimous determination by the jury. And the State of Florida requires unanimity for shoplifting, just not for death. It requires unanimity on all the other elements of the crime. Now, even 簫簫 Apodaca's is a 簫簫 is an unusual decision, as Justice 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, wait a minute. They 簫簫 they 簫簫 they require unanimity for the 簫簫 for a conviction, right?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Yes. And conviction 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Just 簫簫 just 簫簫 just they don't require unanimity on the sentence. That's quite different from 簫簫

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Justice 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: 簫簫 from whether the person committed the crime or not.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Justice Scalia 簫簫 exactly. And, Justice Scalia, leaving aside our Eighth Amendment point in our brief that 簫簫 that followed on Justice Breyer's concurrence in Ring, the 簫簫 this is all about the eligibility, not the determination of what sentence applies. And you have held that the existence of a specified statutory aggravating factor is a condition. It is an element of capital murder, and it is, by statute and Florida Supreme Court decision, an element of capital murder in Florida. And in Apodaca itself, which, as 簫簫 as Justice Thomas pointed out in McDonald, is an extraordinarily unusual case, even there, six justices indicated that a simple majority rule would not pass muster. I mean, we need to 簫簫 once a 簫簫 at 簫簫 when 簫簫 when an assignment is made to a jury in a case to decide beyond a reasonable doubt the existence of an element, however the State defines the element, we need substantial reliability that the jury actually performs those functions. And in this case 簫簫 and, again, in this case, if it were true that the sentencing jury was actually determining death eligibility, which it is plainly not, as we point out, the Eighth Amendment would certainly be violated under Caldwell, because this 簫簫 Florida juries are told that they do not determine death eligibility. And the State simply can't have it both ways. Either the jury is correctly told that its role is merely advisory, in which case there is a Ring violation, or the instruction that it's given violates the Eighth Amendment under Caldwell, because, as in Caldwell, it misleadingly, quote, "minimizes the jury's sense of responsibility for determining the appropriateness of death."

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Waxman, do we 簫簫 do we just treat as irrelevant what was involved in this case, that is, the two aggravators that were alleged, the brutality of the murder, and that it occurred during a robbery? Those were obvious that they existed. Is that not so?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: I think it's not so. It's probably a reason why 簫簫 for 簫簫 I mean, the heinous, atrocious, and cruel aggravator can never be obvious. And the State isn't even arguing harmlessness with respect to that. And as to robbery, I think it's important to 簫簫 to recognize the following: The State made a choice. They didn't even indite Timothy Hurst for robbery. The sentencing jury was not even instructed on the elements of robbery. This argument of harmlessness was never raised in these proceedings from the sentencing proceeding onward, including in the brief in opposition in this case, until the Red brief, and even there the Red brief is simply arguing that there was a fatal concession. But in any event, Justice Ginsburg, there is evidence in the record from which a jury could certainly find that Timothy Hurst, although he was found guilty of first簫degree murder, did not, in fact, actually commit the robbery. The jury was told that to find the existence of the felony murder aggravator, it had to find 簫簫 and I believe this is on Page 211 of the Joint Appendix 簫簫 that it had to find that the murder was committed while he 簫簫 in the course of him committing a robbery. All of the physical evidence in this case that relates to the robbery, the 簫簫 the 簫簫 the bank deposit slip, the money, the bank deposit envelope, and a piece of paper in Lee簫Lee Smith's handwriting toting up the proceeds were all found in Lee簫Lee Smith's possession. And so, although it is not this Court's ordinary function to determine whether something was or wasn't harmless, as in Ring it was remanded for that purpose, I think in this case it manifestly was not harmless. And if there were a remand or any question by this Court on that count, it ought to be remanded to the State court, not only to determine constitutional harmlessness, but whether there was a waiver by the State in its deliberate choice never to mention this either to the second sentencing jury or thereafter.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. Waxman, am 簫簫 am I understanding the case properly? The informant, who had all of the physical evidence, was the main identifier of the defendant, correct?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And 簫簫

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Because there were 簫簫 there were 簫簫 there was an eyewitness from across the street who testified that he saw somebody go into the Popeye's, and he positively identified the defendant.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Now, did 簫簫

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Now, I believe there was another cooperator who backed up Lee簫Lee Smith's testimony.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So the defendant claimed, however, that this informant was the one who did the crime. Could the jury, under the evidence that existed, concluded that they both did it?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And that's why it's debatable whether it's harmless?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Yes. And, in fact 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Because what makes it an aggravator is if he's the one who actually did the killing.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: That he wasn't 簫簫

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, that's what the jury was instructed.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Uh簫huh.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: The jury was instructed that in order to find the felony murder aggravator, it had to find that the murder was committed in the course of him committing the robbery.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Personally.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. That's 簫簫

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: But the 簫簫 the statute, the actual aggravator, is different. But that is what this jury was told.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Waxman, can 簫簫 can I give you a 簫簫 a hypothetical State system? And this is a two簫part question. You tell me if it is consistent with the Sixth Amendment. And if it is, what makes this case different. Okay? So my system is that a jury, whether in the penalty phase or in the guilt phase, has to make a determination of an aggravating factor. Okay? But once that's done, once the jury decides on an aggravating factor, the judge can do whatever she wants. The judge can add aggravating factors. The judge can reweigh the aggravating factors as compared with the mitigating evidence. The judge can do any of that stuff. But the judge has to leave alone the aggravating factor that the jury finds. So, in other words, the judge can't give death when the jury finds life, and the judge can't throw out the jury's factor. But 簫簫 but as long as that jury makes that aggravating factor determination, the judge can do anything. Is that consistent with the Sixth Amendment?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Okay. You're 簫簫 you're asking only about the Sixth Amendment and not the Eighth Amendment 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: 簫簫 point. Okay. So the 簫簫 so just to be sure that I'm specifically answering your question, if the jury is told, you must find 簫簫 for the defendant to be eligible for death, you must find beyond a reasonable doubt the existence of at least one of the statutory aggravating factors. And I would also say for Sixth Amendment purposes, you must either be unanimous or the vote must be at least ten to two. And then the jury does so find. And then you have the sort of belt簫and簫suspenders legal system that the State is positing that Florida has here where the judge can say, okay, I'm the one who does the sentence, so I can weigh the ags and the mits. I can't 簫簫 he is death eligible because the jury found beyond a reasonable doubt that a statutory aggravator exists. But the judge can say, nonetheless, I'm giving life. There's nothing 簫簫 there's no violation of the Sixth Amendment in 簫簫 when that happens. The question is, in this case, when the sentencing jury has concluded its work 簫簫 I mean 簫簫 and I'm assuming in a case where there's not a conviction for a prior aggravated felony. When the sentencing jury has concluded its work, is the defendant eligible for the death penalty under State law? Yes or no. And in Florida, the answer is unquestionably no. Even if we knew that 12 of the jurors found the robbery aggravator here, there would be a Ring violation, just as if we knew that 12 of the jurors found that he had killed the defendant in this case, but they had been told, I just want your input on this because I, the judge, will decide this.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, is that what 簫簫 is that what makes a difference, then, in the end, that 簫簫 that 簫簫 that you're saying that the jury has to be specifically told that that's what it's doing? That 簫簫 and you're saying 簫簫

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: The jury, no.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: 簫簫 the necessary part of a constitutional system for the jury to be instructed that it has the responsibility to find the aggravating factor that serves as a precondition to death.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: At a minimum, if, in fact, the jury is performing that function, it cannot, at least in a capital case, be told that it is not performing that function, that its advice is 簫簫 that its verdict is only advisory.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: What if it's told that it has to decide on life or death, but the judge is 簫簫 if you decide on death, the judge is going to review it, and the judge has the power to sentence to life.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: If the 簫簫 I mean, there is no constitutional violation 簫簫 our view 簫簫 and this again is, is Justice Breyer's Eighth Amendment point, which we endorse. Our view is that capital sentencing always has be 簫簫 has been and, as a matter of constitutional law, should be done by a jury. We're not arguing that other sentences have to be jury sentencing. And so if a jury 簫簫 if a jury says it's death, and the judge says, well, I disagree, I'm only going to sentence him to life, there's no constitutional violation.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, I'm trying to understand the limits of your argument that what is done under the Florida statute diminishes the jury's sense of responsibility. The jury's sense of responsibility will be diminished to some degree if they know that their verdict is not necessarily the final word.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Would that be the case 簫簫 isn't that case whether they 簫簫 whether they're told, you 簫簫 you make a recommendation and the judge decides, or you impose a sentence, but the judge can impose a different sentence, a lesser sentence? There's still 簫簫 they 簫簫 they still don't have to bear the responsibility of making the absolutely final decision.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: So, Justice 簫簫 Justice Alito, let me separate out what I'm calling the selection decision, that is, life or death and the weighing of ags and mits and the eligibility decision, which is all of the elements of capital murder have been found by 簫簫 beyond a reasonable doubt by the jury with either unanimously or a sufficient majority, and, therefore, when the sentencing jury is done, you are eligible for the death penalty. Leaving aside the Eighth Amendment question whether the Constitution then require 簫簫 requires the jury to make the intensely moral judgment about whether the penalty should be life without parole or death, assuming that a judge can do that, so long as the jury is not told that its input, which is how the Florida Supreme Court has put it, is 簫簫 so long as they are not told that it's advisory, so long as they are told that you as the finders of fact have to find that beyond a reasonable doubt that this capital crime was committed, which includes the following elements, including one of the two specifying aggravators, the Constitution is satisfied. The 簫簫 the Caldwell problem is an Eighth Amendment problem. Caldwell was an Eighth Amendment case. And in Caldwell what 簫簫 I mean what the jury is told here 簫簫 if the system exists as the State posits it, what the jury is told here is far more misleading than what was told in Caldwell. In Caldwell, the jury was simply told at closing argument that your decision is going to be reviewable by the Mississippi Supreme Court. And a majority of this Court held that that unconstitutionally diminished the jury's responsibility. Here the jury was told over and over and over again, and consistent with Florida law, that your judgment is merely advisory; I will be the one to make this determination. And either 簫簫 if that isn't 簫簫 that does appear to be the system, that violates Ring. If it isn't the system and if somehow it can be argued that the jury is making implicit findings of aggravation at large, that renders somebody eligible for death, then there is a plain Caldwell problem. And that 簫簫 that's our position. May I reserve the balance of my time?

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: You may.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Mr. Winsor.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Florida's capital sentencing system was constitutional before Ring v. Arizona, and it remains constitutional in light of Ring v. Arizona. What Ring required was a jury determination on those facts on which the State legislature conditions the imposition of the death penalty. In this instance Mr. Hurst got that. The legislature has determined that the elements necessary to make a defendant eligible for the death penalty is the existence of a murder and one or more aggravating circumstances. And what the other side calls the advisory sentence included within it a finding, as this Court recognized in the United States v. Jones, that the jury had determined there was one or more aggravating circumstances. And so the jury 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry. How 簫簫 how is that, when Florida law says that the judge has to find an aggravator to make someone eligibility for the death penalty?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, I agree with the other side that there is a difference between the sentence selection and the sentence eligibility. And so once the defendant is eligible because a jury has found all of the necessary elements, then what happens after that, Your Honor, does not implicate Ring at all.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Could you tell me how this is different than Arizona? I mean, in terms of the system, just like in the Arizona case, there had been precedent by this Court that Arizona law had been constitutional. Unlike Arizona, every Florida 簫簫 every court that has looked 簫簫 every judge who's looked at it, not one of them has said that they believe personally it's constitutional. Even the courts affirming 簫簫 affirm on the basis of the prior precedent, and you have a little less than half the Court directly saying it violates Ring. So what's the jury finding when it says seven to five?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, if I could back up 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Even when it says a murder has been committed, felony murder wasn't. Felony murder was charged, but we don't know if they found the robbery, right?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: We 簫簫 at the guilt phase they convicted of first degree murder, which could have either been felony murder with the predicate underlying felony being robbery or premeditated murder. But to answer your earlier question about the actual 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So how do we know which one they picked?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Which 簫簫 how do we know which one they picked? Which makes them eligible for the death penalty?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, our position is that they 簫簫 that he became eligible at the 簫簫 at the sentencing phase when the jury made its advisory decision, because the jury at that phase was instructed, that if you determine that no aggravating circumstances are found to exist, you must recommend life.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But you do agree that that 簫簫 it doesn't require a unanimous jury?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: It does not require a unanimous jury.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It 簫簫 and 簫簫 a simple majority is all you need?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So we don't have 簫簫

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: That's right, but that's 簫簫 that's a jury finding.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 a unanimous jury, even a functionally equivalent unanimous jury, finding those aggravators.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: We don't have a unanimous or functionally unanimous jury finding those aggravators.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Our reliance for the 簫簫 the final eligibility determination is that seven to five. But I would make this point: The seven to five 簫簫 there are two things that go on when the 簫簫 when the jury determines whether someone should be sentenced to death or not. First, the jury looks and determines whether the State has proven beyond a reasonable doubt an aggravating circumstance. That's the eligibility piece of it. Then they get into the sentence selection process where they weigh the aggravators that they do find, assuming they find some, against the mitigating circumstances. And of course the defendant under this Court's precedent is allowed to put in any evidence that he wishes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry. The jury is not asked to find an aggravator.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It's not asked to find an aggravator.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: It is, Your Honor. It is instructed that it may not return a death recommendation without 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I know. But that's not found at the jury verdict.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It's not found at the trial 簫簫 after 簫簫

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: At the sentencing phase.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You're 簫簫 only at the sentencing phase.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I'm talking about the sentencing phase right 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Suppose that the jury comes back at the sentencing phase and says, we recommend life.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And the reason, though I guess no one would know it, is because they 簫簫 no 簫簫 nobody found an aggravating.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Uh簫huh.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Can the judge, nonetheless, give death.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: No. Not unless 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: With this caveat.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: No, we 簫簫 we have 簫簫 they have a page in their opinion, in their brief, you know, Page 20, where it cites about six Florida cases, which suggested to me that they thought the answer to that question as a matter of Florida law was, yes, the judge can sentence to death.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Yeah. Let me 簫簫 let me 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Is that so or not so?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Let me be clear: As a matter of Florida statutory law, it is permitted. We acknowledge that under Ring it would not be permitted in the circumstance where the State is relying on the recommendation to satisfy the eligibility. Now, you could have a situation 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That's 簫簫 I missed the last part.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: The jury comes back.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Uh簫huh.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: They say life.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And we know, through mental telepathy 簫簫

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 though I guess the judge doesn't, that the reason that they did that is no one found an aggravator. My simple question is: As a matter of Florida law, can the judge impose the death sentence? Yes or no.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: As a matter of Florida statutory law, yes. As a matter of Ring, no. With 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That isn't Florida 簫簫 I mean, it is. It's Federal law. So 簫簫 I mean, Ring is over. So 簫簫 so you say the answer is now no 簫簫

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: With this caveat, Justice Breyer 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 because of Ring. Because you agree that this case is like Ring, and therefore Ring would apply, and therefore 簫簫 not this case, but any case in which they recommend life.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Not any case, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: God, I'd like to know your caveat. What is the caveat? I'm 簫簫 I'm on pins and needles here.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: The caveat is this 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I am, too, actually. Sorry.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: There are multiple ways that a defendant in Florida can become eligible for death. One is, in this case, where it's determined at the sentencing phase because of the finding within the jury's recommendation. In other instances it can be 簫簫 a person can become eligible before the sentencing phase either because they have a prior violent felony conviction or because they have a contemporaneous conviction. For example, if someone murdered two people and were convicted of double murder, that person at the guilt phase, by virtue of that guilt jury's verdict, has been found to be eligible for the death penalty. And so at that stage, then in your hypothetical, Justice Breyer, if that sentencing簫phase jury recommended life, the judge could override it without violating Ring. Now, I will tell you that, as a matter of Florida State law, the judge in that circumstance would face an exacting standard; and, as a matter of fact, no judge has overwritten a jury's life recommendation since before Ring. So as a matter of function, it is just not something that happens in Florida. But to answer your question, we do believe it would be constitutional in the situation that I described.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Suppose the jury came in hung.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: On 簫簫 on the sentence.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: If the sentencing phase 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: The jury 簫簫 jury, instead of being seven to five, it was hung.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: If the 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Could the judge then impose the death penalty?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Not in this situation, Your Honor, because that would result in a life recommendation. A six簫six vote is tantamount to a life recommendation. And the judge could not override that if he were relying on the jury sentencing finding to satisfy Ring. And even if he weren't, he or she weren't, like I said, it's an exacting Florida State law standard. The judge would be reversed for overturning that unless he 簫簫 he or she determined 簫簫 or unless the appellate court determined that no reasonable jury in those circumstances could have imposed or recommended a life sentence. And as I indicated, it's been since 1999 since any judge actually overrode a life recommendation.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Just so I 簫簫 I understand it: So you're saying that it 簫簫 it is possible, but under Florida law the jury would not find the existence of an aggravated 簫簫 aggravating factor but 簫簫 and would 簫簫 and then there are different ways that this would come out 簫簫 the hypothetical was a hung jury 簫簫 but the judge could then proceed to find an aggravating factor and impose the death penalty. Now, you say, now, this hasn't happened. He'd probably be reversed. But theoretically this could happen.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Only 簫簫 that could not happen consistent with Ring, Your Honor, unless there were some other jury finding or admission that established death eligibility.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, you're saying it couldn't happen consistent with Ring, meaning that there are certain applications of the Florida law that would be unconstitutional even in your view.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: That hypothetical that we've explored here 簫簫 again, with their 簫簫 with the absence of another aggravating circumstance proven outside. And 簫簫 and that actually happens in most cases, Justice Kagan.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But 簫簫 but we don't 簫簫 we 簫簫 we don't sit in judgment of 簫簫 of theoretical scheme that Florida has set up, do we?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: No, Your Honor, and 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And don't we have to adjudge that there has been unconstitutionality in this case?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Scalia. And in this case there was a 簫簫 a jury recommendation, actually two jury recommendations 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Could I give you another hypothetical scheme, notwithstanding that we don't sit in judgment of hypothetical schemes?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Suppose that the 簫簫 the 簫簫 the jury finds an aggravating fact, but then, you know, the judge has this whole separate hearing 簫簫 right? 簫簫 in which other things are presented to him. And the judge says, you know, I don't actually agree with the aggravating fact that the jury found, but I have my own aggravating facts, and now I'm 簫簫 I'm doing all the weighing and I come out in favor of death. I 簫簫 I assume that you would say that also would be an unconstitutional application.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: No, Your Honor. That would be consistent with Ring because, again, once death eligibility 簫簫 and 簫簫 and there is a substantial difference that this Court has recognized over the years between the determination of who is eligible for death, and then, of that universe of people eligible for death, for whom is it appropriate?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yes. Quite 簫簫 quite right. But I'm 簫簫 I'm hypothesizing a case in which the jury finds that death簫eligibility marker 簫簫

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: 簫簫 right? If 簫簫 but the judge throws that one out and substitutes his own. That 簫簫 you think that would be constitutional?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, the judge in that instance wouldn't be throwing it out.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: No, he does throw it out. He just says, I don't agree with that. I'm 簫簫 but I'm substituting my own. Would that be all right?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: That would be okay because eligibility would have been determined. Just like if, in my double murder example, the judge believed that, you know, if he were sitting on the jury, maybe he would have acquitted that person of the 簫簫 of the double murder. And of course he can't just override the jury's verdict based on a mere disagreement. In that instance, the death eligibility was determined, not withstanding that he had 簫簫 he'd been the decision maker, maybe would have decided differently. The person is eligible for death. And then it's up to the sentencer.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But I have to say that answer surprises me because the death sentence there is not at all a function of the jury's eligibility finding. The judge has tossed out that eligibility finding and substituted his own, which then leads to the death sentence. So how can we say that that's possibly constitutional under Ring?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Because the point in Ring was to make sure that no person was subject to a greater penalty than they bargained for when they did the crime without a jury finding. And in your hypothetical, the jury finds that there is an aggravator. So there is a jury finding that that person is entitled to the punishment based on the crime that he or she committed.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: The judge has said that that jury finding is utterly irrelevant to his decision about whether to impose death. That he's imposing death based on something that the jury has not found.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: But at that point the judge's determination is separate from the 簫簫 the selection point. The judge is exercising the discretion to sentence within 簫簫 a person who is determined by a jury to be eligible for the death penalty.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That didn't happen here, did it?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: No, your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Good, I thought so 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But the thing is you can't really tell whether that happens in a wide variety of cases. And this is actually 簫簫 this goes to this question of because the jury doesn't actually have to find specific things, only the judge has to find specific things, you often are not going to be able to tell whether the judge's sentence is based on the same aggravating facts that the jury has found.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: But it doesn't need to be under Ring, because once the jury has determined that there is an aggravating factor or if it's been admitted, then the person is death eligible and Ring is completely finished. There's nothing more to do under Ring. And then we move 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Even though the jury is told, now, whatever you say, it's advisory. It's not binding. So you have made a finding of an aggravator, but it's not a binding finder of an aggravator. The jury is told that whatever they say is advisory. Doesn't that make a difference?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: No. What the jury is told is that its ultimate recommendation is 簫簫 is not binding on the Court. And that's true. And that's one of the great benefits of Florida's system. I mean, Florida's system was developed in response to this Court's decision in Ferment, and this Court has said that the Florida's system provides additional benefits to the defendant. So you have a judicial backstop. The matter 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: That was 簫簫 that was before Ring.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: That was before Ring. And we're not contesting that Ring would require a jury finding or an admission of those elements. But once the jury makes its recommendation, even if it recommends death, the judge can override that by 簫簫 for any reason, just based on disagreement alone, which makes it unlike, you know, in a usual capital 簫簫 or the 簫簫 excuse me 簫簫 a usual criminal proceeding where the judge could not overcome 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry. I just want 簫簫 I'm 簫簫 I'm sorry. Justice Scalia.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Is it clear to the jury that they are the last word on whether an aggravator exists or not?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: What it 簫簫 what the jury is told is that they cannot return a death recommendation without finding a 簫簫 an aggravating circumstance. That's 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But then they're also told that the judge is ultimately going to decide whether your recommendation stands or not.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: The judge is going to ultimately impose the sentence, and that's true. And that's both true under Caldwell, but it's not 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But shouldn't it be clear to the jury that 簫簫 that their determination of whether an aggravator exists or not is final? Shouldn't that be clear?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, I 簫簫 I don't think so, Your Honor, because the determination of the aggravator doesn't yield a death sentence unless the judge, in his or her own opinion, believes the death is appropriate. That's a benefit.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But I'm 簫簫 I'm talking 簫簫 I'm talking about what responsibility the jury feels. If the jury knows that if 簫簫 if 簫簫 if we don't 簫簫 if 簫簫 if we don't find it an aggravator, it can't be found; or if we do find an aggravator, it must be accepted. That's a lot more responsibility than just, you know, well, you know, if you find an aggravator and you 簫簫 you weigh it and provide for the death penalty, the judge is going to review it anyway.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I'm not sure that's an accurate characterization of what goes on because it's not that the judge must accept 簫簫 the aggravator determination has no purpose or no point other than determining eligibility and then the weighing. And if the judge determines that the death sentence is not appropriate for whatever reason, then the fact that the jury found an aggravating circumstance makes no difference.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Suppose then in your earlier hypothetical, the judge 簫簫 the jury finds an aggravator occurred in the course of the robbery, and, therefore, there is death eligibility. Then it goes to the judge. And the judge says, there is simply no evidence to support that aggravating factor, but I find another aggravating factor. Under your view, the judge could go ahead and impose the death penalty?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, in that instance, that's a little bit different, as I understand it, than Justice Kagan's hypothetical. First of all, the 簫簫 the recommendation doesn't specify what 簫簫 which of the aggravating factors 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: But this is my hypothetical.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Okay. So to make sure I understand 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I mean, a death case, which is not funny.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Which honestly sounds the same.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, I think the 簫簫 I think the difference is, respectfully, is his included the 簫簫 the finding that the judge finds no evidence to support as opposed to just disagreeing with their 簫簫 excuse me.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: And what would happen?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: If you had a situation 簫簫 and, again, this would be limited 簫簫 let me make sure I'm limiting the 簫簫 the answer to the situation where the State is depending on the death recommendation of finding an aggravator.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Which is the minority of cases, as we 簫簫 as we said in the brief. If the jury made a specific finding as to a specific aggravator 簫簫 and, again, they wouldn't be instructed on that aggravator unless there was sufficient evidence of it at the 簫簫 at the threshold stage. But if the judge concluded that there was insufficient evidence 簫簫 again, he never would have submitted it to the jury 簫簫 then that 簫簫 that probably would not be permissible.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Go for a second to an analogy.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: There's 簫簫 there are two good other analogies in other areas of the law. The one that supports you is robbery, force or threat of force. No one ever asks or needn't ask whether six members of the jury thought there was a threat but no actual, or seven members thought there was actual but no threat. Threat or threat of force. I don't think we have to say. I don't think so. So that supports you. On the other hand, imagine a normal sentencing case. The statute says, you 簫簫 you get aggravated punishment if you had 50 grams of cocaine. The jury finds he had 50 grams of cocaine 簫簫 no. Sorry. The statute says aggravated sentence if 50 grams of cocaine or meth. The jury finds he had 50 grams of cocaine. I don't think under Apprendi, which I didn't agree with 簫簫 but I don't think the judge could say, I'm going to give you the aggravated sentence because I don't believe there was any cocaine, but I do believe there was meth.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: That may well be right. And that's one of the reasons the jury is not asked to find specific aggravating factors.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But we do know that the judge here 簫簫 now still you're having conceded Ring, where the jury says no aggravating factor. We know that the jury can, if the jury finds aggravating factor X, have death on a completely different aggravating factor, but the jury never thought of, namely why. Now, we know that. And now compare that to the hypothetical of cocaine and meth. And then we have Apprendi, which I disagree with still, but...

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, I think, Your Honor, in the cocaine and meth example, I believe that the 簫簫 the Court would look, as they did in Jones, to say, well, is the legislature setting this up as distinctive offenses, or are they setting this up as one offense that can be satisfied either by possession of cocaine or meth. And if it were the latter, then the jury would just be instructed to find one or the other without any 簫簫 any specific 簫簫 and we know is that 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I want to clarify. You think a seven簫to簫five recommendation is finding an element of the crime that makes you eligible for the death penalty by a unanimous or functionally equivalent unanimous jury?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: We do, Your Honor. And let me say 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, then what do you do with the statement in our case law that says a simple majority is not a unanimous jury?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, we don't say that it's a unanimous jury. Let me step back and say that 簫簫 that the 簫簫 the seven簫to簫five vote, by the way, is not necessarily five votes that there was no aggravating circumstance, because, again, it's 簫簫 there's two things that go on in the jury room. One, they decide whether there were aggravating circumstances. And, two, they just 簫簫 they do 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And they don't agree with which one?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Sorry?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So we don't know whether it was premeditation or robbery. It could be four to three or two to five. It could be anything.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I'm talking about the jury 簫簫 in the sentencing phase now.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: So a seven to five could well mean that all 12 jurors found a robbery and all 12 jurors found heinous, atrociousness, and cruel 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: We don't know. What does the seven to five tell us the jury found?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: The seven to five tells us that at a minimum, a majority of the jury at a minimum, found beyond a reasonable doubt that the State had proven the existence of one or more aggravating circumstances. And getting back to Justice 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Not the same one?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Not the same?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Not the same one. And again, getting back to Justice Breyer's point about the cocaine and the meth, the courts in these situations look at what the 簫簫 what the legislature's 簫簫 its definitions of the element. And we know, as a matter of Florida State law, that the element at issue here, to take someone who is not eligible for the death penalty and to make him or her eligible for the death penalty is the existence of one or more aggravating circumstances, not 簫簫 not a specific one. And so it is like Schad v. Arizona, where you 簫簫 you can't say whether the jury agreed that there was premeditation or whether there was felony murder. And, in fact, that was the case at this defendant's guilt phase back in 1998. He was convicted of first簫degree murder. And the guilt phase jury was instructed that they could return that verdict either by finding premeditation or by finding felony murder. And there was no jury finding as to which one 簫簫 which one it was. And so as a matter of 簫簫 I think to answer your question about whether they all need to be the same or not, it would depend on what the 簫簫 how the State legislature 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But you believe that a simple majority is a jury unanimously or functionally unanimously finding that element beyond a reasonable doubt?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, we 簫簫 they're certainly finding it beyond a reasonable doubt. And we're relying on the Court's decision in Apodaca, but 簫簫 but also 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: That was ten to two.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Does ten to two automatically mean that seven to five is okay?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Not automatically, Your Honor. But I think if you look at Apodaca, what they were rejecting was the same arguments that the petitioner is asking this Court to accept, which is that this long history of the unanimity and the 12簫person jury necessarily means it's 簫簫 it's brought in to 簫簫 to our system. But I'll say this too, the seven to five is not the same kind of jury verdict that you'd have at a 簫簫 at a guilt phase because of this judicial backstop, because of the other protections that Florida has put in in place. And so even if it's a seven簫to簫five vote, you still have the judge coming behind that jury, who 簫簫 unlike at the guilt phase where he must accept the jury's findings, unless they're not supported by evidence, he or she can disagree for any reason. He or she can give mercy for any reason. And that happens a lot. And so we've cited the 簫簫 some cases in our brief where a man was convicted of murder in a horrible sexual assault, and by virtue of those two convictions was necessarily eligible for the death penalty. The jury heard all of the evidence, made a recommendation that he receive the death penalty, and the judge said, no, I'm going to sentence him to life. And so this Court 簫簫 you know, this gets back into the jury versus judge sentencing. But there are some real benefits associated with judicial sentences. And if you go back to Proffitt, when this Court first upheld Florida's capital sentencing system, it recognized the advantages of judicial sentencing because you're not going to have someone's life or death being determined exclusively on the 簫簫 perhaps the 簫簫 the emotions of a jury.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Can 簫簫 can I go back to the kinds of hypotheticals that Justice Kennedy and I were 簫簫 were proposing? So let's say that there is a jury, and 簫簫 and it's been presented with evidence that the murder was for pecuniary gain, which is one of the aggravating factors. And the 簫簫 the 簫簫 the jury comes out with a recommendation of death. So you 簫簫 and that was the only thing that was presented to it. So you know that the 簫簫 the jury has made a death eligibility determination on pecuniary gain. Then it goes to the judge. The judge says, you know what, I don't really think that there's enough evidence of pecuniary gain, but I've had this whole hearing, and I find that the thing was 簫簫 that the crime was heinous and whatnot. And now I'm going to sentence the person to death. Now, you say that that's fine; is that right?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, let me 簫簫 let me 簫簫 I realize it's a hypothetical, but let me tell you why that couldn't happen in Florida. A judge would not instruct a jury on an aggravating circumstance for which there was not sufficient evidence to find that. And so your hypothetical would not happen if there 簫簫 there was 16 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, you know 簫簫 no 簫簫 he's 簫簫 well 簫簫 he's heard more evidence because, you know, there's a whole new hearing that he has. And now he's considered it more thoroughly, and he thinks, no, I don't agree with that anymore, but I think it was heinous. So that would be fine.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Again, that's not this case because there was no additional evidence 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yes, yes, yes, it's not this case.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: But 簫簫 but the 簫簫 the 簫簫 the 簫簫 if the 簫簫 if the judge found that the 簫簫 that there was no evidence of any aggravator 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I'm throwing out 簫簫

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: 簫簫 the jury's aggravating factor, but I'm substituting my own. I thought that that was what you told me that that was constitutional under Ring.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, I think it depends on 簫簫 on why you're throwing it out. If there 簫簫 if 簫簫 if 簫簫 as with any jury finding, if a judge finds at the guilt phase that there is insufficient evidence to find any element, then 簫簫 then he would not rely on the 簫簫 the jury's determination there.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: This wasn't at the guilt phase; this was just as a matter of sentencing. Let me get on with my questioning 簫簫

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Yeah. Sure.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: 簫簫 because I think you answered this one already. Then the appeal that's taken, right 簫簫 the appeal is focusing now only on what the judge has found, isn't that right, under Florida law? The appeal 簫簫 if the person came in and said that there was insufficient evidence, the appeal would only be as to the judge's finding and not at all to the jury's.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, the 簫簫 if I understand the hypothetical correctly, someone's convicted, has a death recommendation, a death sentence, and is appealing to the Florida Supreme Court.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: And 簫簫 and he says there was just not enough evidence of all these aggravating factors, so 簫簫 but that was 簫簫 would only be as to the judge's aggravating factors. It couldn't possibly be that he would challenge the jury's.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, the judge's aggravating factors would be detailed in a written order. But if it 簫簫 if there were 簫簫 if 簫簫 if 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I mean, I'm just suggesting that the whole appeal process suggests that the crucial death eligibility determination is being made by the judge because that's the only death eligibility determination that the appeals court is ever going to review.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, I think that's 簫簫 gets to another benefit of Florida's system, is that 簫簫 that they 簫簫 they do have this to review. You know, there's been some suggestion of jury sentencing as a 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yes, look, they have something to review. The problem is it's the judge's thing to 簫簫 that they're reviewing, not the jury's, and that's a Sixth Amendment problem.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I don't think it's a Sixth Amendment problem any more than 簫簫 than here when you. At the 簫簫 at the 簫簫 at the guilt phase when he appealed and there was a 簫簫 an examination of the evidence and they didn't know whether the jury found on felony murder predicate or on first簫degree murder. They're reviewing the conviction, and they're reviewing the evidence that sustains it.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: What about 簫簫

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Or they may sustain it.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: So to what degree is there 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: How about if that jury 簫簫 I'm sorry 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: So to what degree is there a 簫簫 a real dispute here about the presence of the two aggravating factors?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: There is none, Justice Alito, in my view. And I know that there was some argument a moment ago about the 簫簫 about the evidence suggesting that someone else may have committed the crime. We cited in our brief from their 簫簫 initial brief in the Florida Supreme Court where they said, "Without any contention, this is a two簫aggravator case. Hurst does not challenge the trial court's findings that the murder was committed during the course of a robbery, and it was especially heinous, atrocious, or cruel." He doesn't question the seriousness of those aggravators either. His focus instead, acknowledging all that, was on proportionality review, which is not at issue here, but which, by the way, is another benefit of the Florida system, that the Florida Supreme Court reviews everything for 簫簫 for proportionality.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Can I ask you two separate questions on this?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Number one, whenever have we said that a jury waiver on an issue is based on the lack of a challenge by a defense attorney? Don't we require waivers of jury trials to be explicit and by the defendant him or herself?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: When someone's waiving the jury trial altogether, absolutely. And, of course, that would be structural error even if there were no objection. But this is 簫簫 this is 簫簫 goes to more like the 簫簫 the element of offense. And the Court held in Washington v. Recuenco that the Apprendi error is subject to harmless error here.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So where have we ever said that not challenging something is an admission of that something?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: We take plenty of appeals where people are saying, assuming the state of facts, I'm entitled to X. And then when they go back down, they argue that that goes 簫簫 that assumption is wrong. Why isn't this the same?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Well, we 簫簫 we cited other portions of the 簫簫 where they had 簫簫 excuse me 簫簫 acknowledged that below back in the sentencing memorandum of the first go簫around. But to follow up on your question, Justice Alito, about whether there is an existence of a 簫簫 of a doubt, the Florida Supreme Court found that both of these clearly existed at the post簫conviction opinion, which led to the resentencing that's now on appeal here. They sent it back for resentencing not because of anything having to do with death eligibility or the establishment of aggravators. They sent it back because there was insufficient effort to produce mitigating 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Has there ever been an appeal in Florida where an advisory jury was given 簫簫 not given a proper instruction and a resentence was 簫簫 was ordered for that reason?

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: Has there been a Florida Supreme Court reversing a death sentence for 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: An improper instruction to the advisory jury.

turns.name: WINSOR
turns.text: I'd be surprised if there weren't, Your Honor, but I 簫簫 I don't know. I 簫簫 I'll 簫簫 I'll 簫簫 I'll look at that. Getting back to the 簫簫 to the admission, the evidence was clear. There is no question that there was a robbery here. There is no question that there was 簫簫 that this was heinous, atrocious, and cruel. And we would ask that the Court affirm the Florida Supreme Court's judgment.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Waxman, six minutes.

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Notwithstanding this flurry of papers, I'm aspiring not to use the six minutes. Let me first 簫簫 let me go right to Justice Scalia's question, which is not a hypothetical, although I'm happy to answer hypotheticals. Your question was: Was the jury told and doesn't a jury have to be told that as to death eligibility, the element of the crime of capital murder, that it makes the decision? The answer is: It does have to be told that. It certainly can't be told the opposite, and it absolutely was not told that. It was told over and over again, consistent with the statute, that its decision was purely advisory. And I want to refer the Court to the Florida Supreme Court's decision in State v. Steele, which is at 921 So. The Florida Supreme Court in Steele said, first of all, "Nothing in the statute, the standard jury instructions, or the standard verdict form requires a majority of the jury to agree on which aggravating circumstances exist. Under current law, the jury may recommend a sentence of death where four jurors believe only that one aggravator applies, while three others believe that only another aggravator applies, because seven jurors believe that at least one aggravator applies." Florida goes beyond that. It 簫簫 it is unlawful 簫簫 and the Supreme Court of Florida has said 簫簫 to require 簫簫 to ask the jury, the sentencing jury, to provide a special verdict that in any way indicates what their, quote, input is on the sentencing factors. Again, Steele, at page 546. "Specific jury findings on aggravators, without guidance about their effect on the imposition of a sentence, could unduly influence the trial judge's own determination of how to sentence the defendant. The trial court alone must make detailed findings about the existence and weight of aggravating circumstances."

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Is that a 簫簫 post Ring? What's the date of that?

turns.name: WAXMAN
turns.text: Yes, this is post Ring. And the Court also held that Ring didn't apply. It has no jury findings on which to rely. And, in fact, the Court also explained in 簫簫 later in the decision 簫簫 in the same decision and also in its decision in Franklin, that Florida bar 簫簫 quote, "Florida bars a special verdict precisely because requiring specific jury findings on aggravators, without guidance about their effect, would harm the jury's independent" 簫簫 "the trial court's independent determination." Now, counsel 簫簫 my 簫簫 my colleague on the other side here says that, well, there 簫簫 there would not be a statutory problem, but there would be a Ring problem if we knew that the jury found that no aggravators existed. So how can Ring be satisfied when we have no earthly idea what the jury found? It could be, as in this case, as Steele acknowledges, three for one and four for the other. As to the, I think, hypothetical question that Justice Kagan was asking 簫簫 so, you know, in a circumstance, how much leeway does the judge have to make his own 簫簫 his or her own decisions on the death penalty, the Florida Supreme Court has specifically allowed the death penalty to be imposed and a determination of death eligibility to be made based on evidence that was never presented to the sentencing jury and based on an aggravating factor on which the sentencing jury was not applied. And the notion that there hasn't been a life簫override since Ring is 簫簫 is an interesting fact, but this Court, in this Court's Sposiano decision, in this Court's Daubert decision, that's exactly what happened: The jury said, we want life. The judge said, I'm hearing 簫簫 I'm hearing independent evidence, and you're getting death. Now, as to the supposed concessions in this case, I think I'll rely largely on our brief. But the notion that somebody 簫簫 that the lawyers said this is a two簫aggravator case is certainly true. There were two aggravators charged. And maybe the jury 簫簫 we know that the trial judge found that two aggravators were satisfied. This defendant has been making the Ring argument since before Ring was decided. He raised this as an Apprendi issue at the very first trial. He asked for a bill of particulars for the 簫簫 the State to indicate which aggravators it was going to rely on, and he was denied on the grounds that Apprendi doesn't apply. Even the 簫簫 again, the central Ring problem in this case, the central Sixth Amendment problem in this case, leaving aside the indeterminacy of seven to five 簫簫 and maybe it's three for one and four for the other 簫簫 is that, when a Florida sentencing jury finishes its work, there is simply no question: The defendant is not eligible for the death penalty. Only the trial judge can do that. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
